paragraphs.text: AEPDV (Portuguese Video Game Publishers Association) represents publishers, distributors, and console manufacturers in Portugal, while the country's video game developers are represented by APVP (Portuguese Video Game Producers Association). Both organisations work closely together on the most relevant national and European matters, particularly through their cooperation with Video Games Europe (VGE) and the European Games Developer Federation (EGDF).

section_path: Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: As a non-profit association, AEPDV's mission is to promote the sustainable growth of the Portuguese video games industry, foster dialogue with public authorities, and advocate for balanced policies that enhance both competitiveness and consumer protection.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: In 2024, the Portuguese video games sector employed around 1,900 professionals across more than 100 development studios, with an aggregate turnover estimated at €100 million. In parallel, the national video games consumer market exceeded €300 million, reflecting the growing relevance of this creative and technological industry within the Portuguese economy.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: notably PEGI and USK, which have proven effective in safeguarding minors, ensuring transparency, and promoting digital literacy.

paragraphs.text: AEPDV fully supports the position of VGE and EGDF, emphasising that the Digital Fairness Act should prioritise the consistent enforcement of existing legislation (UCPD, DSA, DMA, AI Act) and strengthen self- and co-regulatory mechanisms, notably PEGI and USK, which have proven effective in safeguarding minors, ensuring transparency, and promoting digital literacy.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: Portugal's video games ecosystem therefore shares the European commitment to securing a fair, safe, and proportionate digital environment, where innovation and consumer protection coexist in a balanced and sustainable way.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: AEPDV informs parents and players about responsible game play via #SaberJogar for more than 8 years ago.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: Thus, AEPDV welcomes the European Commission's consultation on the forthcoming Digital Fairness Act and the opportunity to submit our feedback as outlined below.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: We fully support the goal of ensuring a fair, transparent and safe digital environment for consumers. Our industry shares this objective and has been proactively improving transparency, fairness, and player protection for more than two decades.

section_path: Maintaining an Effective and Balanced Consumer Framework

evidence_quotes.quote: The European video games sector set up in 2003 a pan-European system of self- and co-regulation for minor protection – the PEGI (Pan-European Game Information) system –

paragraphs.text: The European video games sector set up in 2003 a pan-European system of self- and co-regulation for minor protection – the PEGI (Pan-European Game Information) system –

section_path: Maintaining an Effective and Balanced Consumer Framework

evidence_quotes.quote: PEGI, alongside national frameworks such as Germany's USK, ensures a high level of protection for minors through enforceable codes of conduct, labelling, and parental tools.

paragraphs.text: created at the request of the EU institutions and used in over 40 countries, PEGI, alongside national frameworks such as Germany's USK, ensures a high level of protection for minors through enforceable codes of conduct, labelling, and parental tools.

section_path: Maintaining an Effective and Balanced Consumer Framework

paragraphs.text: We believe that the existing EU Consumer Protection Acquis remains fit for purpose. Its horizontal, principle-based framework provides the flexibility needed to address digital consumer challenges. Instead of introducing new, prescriptive legislation, the EU should focus on consistent enforcement of existing and newly adopted laws, including the Digital Services Act, Digital Markets Act and AI Act.

section_path: Maintaining an Effective and Balanced Consumer Framework

paragraphs.text: Any new initiative must avoid measures that would stifle innovation, disrupt legitimate business models, reduce consumer choice or undermine Europe's competitiveness in a global market. We also call for clear and explicit recognition of the proven effectiveness of self- and co-regulation as regards minor protection and information to the consumers.

section_path: Maintaining an Effective and Balanced Consumer Framework

evidence_quotes.quote: Purchases of content to use in the game such as in-game currencies (IGCs) have long been part of video games and are correctly classified in EU law as "digital content" according to the Digital Content Directive (DCD).

paragraphs.text: Purchases of content to use in the game such as in-game currencies (IGCs) have long been part of video games and are correctly classified in EU law as "digital content" according to the Digital Content Directive (DCD). This digital content exists only within games, have no real-world monetary value and are fully covered by the existing consumer protection acquis. Reclassifying IGCs as "digital representations of value" would mislead consumers, reduce their protections, and create significant compliance burdens for developers, particularly smaller European studios.

section_path: Maintaining an Effective and Balanced Consumer Framework
Ensuring Transparency and Fairness in Purchases

evidence_quotes.quote: Key Principles on Virtual In-game Currencies

paragraphs.text: To increase transparency and consumer understanding, Video Games Europe and the European Games Developers Federation (EGDF) have developed proposals addressing the concerns of the CPC Network's 'Key Principles on Virtual In-game Currencies'. These include:

section_path: Maintaining an Effective and Balanced Consumer Framework
Ensuring Transparency and Fairness in Purchases

evidence_quotes.quote: Clear information to help players understand the real-world value of IGCs;

paragraphs.text: Clear information to help players understand the real-world value of IGCs;

section_path: Maintaining an Effective and Balanced Consumer Framework
Ensuring Transparency and Fairness in Purchases

paragraphs.text: Easy access to transaction histories;

section_path: Maintaining an Effective and Balanced Consumer Framework
Ensuring Transparency and Fairness in Purchases

evidence_quotes.quote: IGC bundles designed to minimise leftover balances;

paragraphs.text: IGC bundles designed to minimise leftover balances;

section_path: Maintaining an Effective and Balanced Consumer Framework
Ensuring Transparency and Fairness in Purchases

evidence_quotes.quote: Fair refund options for unused purchases

paragraphs.text: Fair refund options for unused purchases and unauthorised transactions by minors;

section_path: Maintaining an Effective and Balanced Consumer Framework
Ensuring Transparency and Fairness in Purchases

paragraphs.text: A minimum 30-day notice before removing paid in-game content; and

section_path: Maintaining an Effective and Balanced Consumer Framework
Ensuring Transparency and Fairness in Purchases

paragraphs.text: Ongoing improvements to parental tools and age-appropriate design.

section_path: Maintaining an Effective and Balanced Consumer Framework
Ensuring Transparency and Fairness in Purchases

paragraphs.text: These measures ensure transparency and fairness without disrupting the legal framework or innovation in game design, and without depriving consumers of their existing rights under the DCD.

section_path: Maintaining an Effective and Balanced Consumer Framework
Ensuring Transparency and Fairness in Purchases

paragraphs.text: The European Commission's Better Regulation agenda stated clearly that "principle-based legislation requires well-designed complementary non-regulatory actions to deliver effectively on its goals. In this new better regulation landscape, the Principles for better self- and co-regulation are endorsed as benchmarks for the good practice of Self-Regulation/Co-Regulation".

section_path: Recognising the Value of Self- and Co-Regulation that actively addresses new risks

evidence_quotes.quote: The Pan-European Game Information (PEGI) system, established at the request of EU institutions, has for over two decades provided a harmonised framework for age ratings and online safety.

paragraphs.text: Our sector has built strong and effective systems of self- and co-regulation that complement the principle based EU law to ensure player protection in practice. The Pan-European Game Information (PEGI) system, established at the request of EU institutions, has for over two decades provided a harmonised framework for age ratings and online safety.

section_path: Recognising the Value of Self- and Co-Regulation that actively addresses new risks

paragraphs.text: PEGI's enforceable Code of Conduct is regularly updated to reflect technological and social developments. Recent updates end 2023 introduced stronger transparency rules for purchases of digital content and randomised content, including measures against skin gambling and unauthorised trading, and clear information requirements for purchases. Breaches of the Code can lead to corrective actions and sanctions imposed by an independent enforcement committee.

section_path: Recognising the Value of Self- and Co-Regulation that actively addresses new risks

evidence_quotes.quote: offering easy-to-use parental tools allowing to block spending or set limits should a parent decide to allow purchases of digital content.

paragraphs.text: Members of the two European trade bodies of which AEPDV is a member have also committed to additional good-practice principles, such as clear refund policies when a purchase has been made without the explicit consent of a parent, timely communication of product changes, separating gameplay from commercial transactions, and offering easy-to-use parental tools allowing to block spending or set limits should a parent decide to allow purchases of digital content.

section_path: Recognising the Value of Self- and Co-Regulation that actively addresses new risks

paragraphs.text: These systems demonstrate that regulated self and co-regulation can respond to concerns more flexibly than legislation, while ensuring high levels of consumer protection and supporting innovation and competitiveness.

section_path: Recognising the Value of Self- and Co-Regulation that actively addresses new risks

paragraphs.text: We are concerned that the European Commission in its report on the Digital Fairness Fitness check did not list these specific advancements. We encourage the European Commission to consider the significant role that the existing PEGI and USK frameworks play in the video game sector as these are able to address sector specific concerns in a fast and flexible manner than legislative proposals at EU level. As minor protection is the key element in the recently adopted DSA guidelines on Article 28, we encourage DG CNCT to engage in further dialogue with the video game sector regarding the status of the existing co-regulatory framework to address the concerns that DG JUST has raised in its consultation, as these predominantly address minor protection.

section_path: Recognising the Value of Self- and Co-Regulation that actively addresses new risks

evidence_quotes.quote: Optional purchases of in-game content, including IGCs and randomised rewards, are popular, lawful and transparent features enjoyed by millions of players.

paragraphs.text: We caution against disproportionate or prescriptive measures that would undermine legitimate business models or consumer choice. Optional purchases of in-game content, including IGCs and randomised rewards, are popular, lawful and transparent features enjoyed by millions of players. When implemented responsibly, they are not manipulative or unfair.

section_path: Avoiding Disproportionate Regulatory Intervention

evidence_quotes.quote: Optional purchases of digital content is the leading business model of free to play games, which are very popular among consumers, as today 71 % play on mobile.

paragraphs.text: Banning or severely restricting these features would harm European developers and consumers alike. Optional purchases of digital content is the leading business model of free to play games, which are very popular among consumers, as today 71 % play on mobile. Optional purchases also support games that are not free to play, and support the continuous development of games, including non-mobiles games. Excessive and detailed intervention in business models would create barriers for small studios, discourage investment, and risk pushing innovation outside the EU.

section_path: Avoiding Disproportionate Regulatory Intervention

paragraphs.text: Our sector's experience shows that existing consumer law, the PEGI framework, and additional proposals made already address concerns around pricing transparency, randomised content and child protection effectively and proportionately.

section_path: Avoiding Disproportionate Regulatory Intervention

paragraphs.text: We welcome the Commission's attention to consumer protection in digital markets, but believe that many proposed measures in the consultation are already addressed through existing law or industry practice. In particular:

section_path: Clarifying the Approach to Specific Policy Measures

evidence_quotes.quote: Real-world prices for IGCs can be indicated approximately but not precisely, due to bundle variations and multiple sales channels.

paragraphs.text: **Price transparency** – Real-world prices for IGCs can be indicated approximately but not precisely, due to bundle variations and multiple sales channels. Focus should be on clear information on how players can calculate the equivalent value ensures transparency without misleading consumers. Members of Video Games Europe and EGDF presented industry commitments last year, and more recently additional proposals to the CPC Network, which address the concerns related to price transparency without the need for additional legislation.

section_path: Clarifying the Approach to Specific Policy Measures

paragraphs.text: **Probabilities for random rewards** – Disclosure of probabilities is standard practice at platform and publisher level and is now required under the PEGI Code of Conduct and the German USK system. The PEGI code of Conduct roles should be referred to as standards to be adopted for all games made available in Europe.

section_path: Clarifying the Approach to Specific Policy Measures

evidence_quotes.quote: default settings for child accounts typically set spending to zero
how refunds can be available for unauthorised transactions

paragraphs.text: **Parental control and spending limits** – All major platforms provide tools to block or limit purchases. The Commission should reflect on default settings for child accounts typically set spending to zero, and how refunds can be available for unauthorised transactions.

section_path: Clarifying the Approach to Specific Policy Measures

paragraphs.text: **Dark patterns** – Existing EU laws, including the UCPD, GDPR, DSA and AI Act, already prohibit manipulative design. The Commission should focus on issuing practical guidance

section_path: Clarifying the Approach to Specific Policy Measures

paragraphs.text: with examples of compliant design, rather than adding new rules.

section_path: Clarifying the Approach to Specific Policy Measures

paragraphs.text: **Addictive design** – Terms such as “addictive features” are vague and unscientific. Features that encourage engagement, such as rewards or progress mechanics, should not be restricted unless evidence of harm exists. Parental tools and education are more effective.

section_path: Clarifying the Approach to Specific Policy Measures

evidence_quotes.quote: Current systems already allow users to cancel subscriptions securely.

paragraphs.text: **Digital contracts** – Current systems already allow users to cancel subscriptions securely. Mandatory cancellation buttons outside secure accounts would create unnecessary risks and have proven little used in practice.

section_path: Clarifying the Approach to Specific Policy Measures

evidence_quotes.quote: using age assurance methods to exclude minors entirely from certain digital products

paragraphs.text: **Age assurance** – using age assurance methods to exclude minors entirely from certain digital products is not an appropriate and proportionate measure, and will push them to either circumvent such measures or towards less regulated experiences. Such an approach is out of line with children’s right to have equal and effective access to the digital environment in ways that are meaningful for them such as culture, leisure and play.

section_path: Clarifying the Approach to Specific Policy Measures

items.topic: Burden Of Proof And Consumer Definition

evidence_quotes.quote: Burden of proof and consumer definition
Expanding these principles would create legal uncertainty and increase litigation.
The current definitions already strike an appropriate balance between protection and predictability.

paragraphs.text: **Burden of proof and consumer definition** – Expanding these principles would create legal uncertainty and increase litigation. The current definitions already strike an appropriate balance between protection and predictability.

section_path: Clarifying the Approach to Specific Policy Measures

paragraphs.text: Protecting minors is a long-standing and central commitment of the video games industry. Through age-rating systems such as PEGI, the German USK, and the International Age Rating Coalition (IARC), players and parents are provided with clear, reliable information about age suitability, content and online interactions.

section_path: Promoting Child Protection and Parental Involvement

evidence_quotes.quote: online environments are safe by design

paragraphs.text: Our sector follows strict rules to ensure that online environments are safe by design, including effective moderation, reporting mechanisms and sanctions for breaches. Parental control tools on all major platforms enable parents to manage their children’s access to content, communication features and in-game spending.

section_path: Promoting Child Protection and Parental Involvement

paragraphs.text: The industry actively promotes awareness campaigns in cooperation with public authorities and family organisations to encourage parents to use these tools and to engage with their children’s gaming activities. This shared-responsibility model, based on cooperation between industry, regulators and parents, should serve as an example for future EU child-protection initiatives in the digital environment.

section_path: Promoting Child Protection and Parental Involvement

paragraphs.text: We welcome initiatives taken by the Ministry of Digitalisation in Denmark. The Ministry published a new agreement with the Telecommunications industry as well as civil society on helping parents guide children with their first phones. Parents will receive guidance at the point of purchase, both online and in physical stores, on how to set up devices with age-appropriate settings.

section_path: Promoting Child Protection and Parental Involvement

paragraphs.text: We encourage the European Commission to build the Digital Fairness Act on the existing strong foundations of EU consumer law and successful self and co-regulatory frameworks.

section_path: Conclusion and Recommendations

paragraphs.text: We recommend that EU policymakers:

section_path: Conclusion and Recommendations

paragraphs.text: Focus on consistent enforcement and practical guidance rather than new legislation;

section_path: Conclusion and Recommendations

paragraphs.text: Recognise and strengthen effective self and co-regulatory systems and endorse industry proposals to address the concerns related to price transparency.

section_path: Conclusion and Recommendations

paragraphs.text: Avoid disproportionate or prescriptive measures that would harm innovation, SMEs and consumer choice;

section_path: Conclusion and Recommendations

paragraphs.text: Significantly support education and awareness initiatives that empower parents and players; and

section_path: Conclusion and Recommendations

paragraphs.text: Uphold legal certainty, proportionality and competitiveness as guiding principles.

section_path: Conclusion and Recommendations

paragraphs.text: By taking a balanced, evidence-based approach, the EU can ensure digital fairness while supporting a vibrant, innovative and globally competitive European video games sector.

section_path: Conclusion and Recommendations

paragraphs.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
